checklist.item: Include a concept recap on Python reserved words and their usage in code.
checklist.reason: The slides emphasize reserved words as foundational knowledge, and the student benefits from concrete, step-by-step explanations.

checklist.item: Provide syntax practice for writing and debugging print statements with correct string formatting.
checklist.reason: The slides highlight print syntax errors as a common issue, and the student requires hands-on reinforcement to avoid abstract theory.

lab.title: Python Reserved Words & Print Syntax Practice
lab.intro: Let's explore Python's reserved words and practice writing clean print statements. We'll use hands-on examples and debugging exercises to reinforce these concepts.
lab.tutor_opening: I'll show you how reserved words work, then you'll practice identifying and using them. We'll fix some print statement errors together!

cells.title: Python Reserved Words Overview
cells.narrative: Python has 35 reserved words that cannot be used as variable names. They control program flow and define syntax structure.
cells.code:
import keyword
print('Reserved words list:', keyword.kwlist)
cells.tutor_script: Run the code to see all reserved words. Notice how they control program logic like 'if' and 'while'.

cells.title: Identify Reserved Words
cells.task_prompt: Which of these are valid variable names? Test by uncommenting each line
success_criteria: Correctly identify which names cause errors
Comment out invalid options with explanations

cells.title: Print Statement Syntax
cells.narrative: Proper string formatting prevents syntax errors. Here's correct usage with different formats:
cells.code:
name = 'Python'
print(f'Learning {name}')  # f-string
print('Count:', 42)      # Comma separation
print('Sum is ' + str(2+2))  # String concatenation
cells.tutor_script: Notice how each format handles variable insertion differently. The comma adds space automatically!

cells.title: Fix Syntax Errors
cells.task_prompt: Correct the broken print statements below
cells.starter_code:
print('Hello'  # Missing closing quote
print('Result is' + 7)  # Needs string conversion
print('Average:', 3.14  # Missing closing parenthesis)

cells.title: Formatted Output Example
cells.narrative: This shows proper formatting for multiple data types:
cells.tutor_script: The f-string inserts variables directly while % formatting limits decimal places.

lab.completion_message: You've practiced identifying reserved words and fixed print statement syntax! Next, we'll explore function definitions using these concepts.
